question: What visual diagram is shown on the blackboard when the lecture first introduces vectors a and b, and what equation does the perpendicularity condition for that diagram ultimately lead to for solving x?
answer: The blackboard shows two arrows labeled 'a' and 'b' representing vectors with a right-angle indicator showing the projection geometry. The perpendicularity condition that the error e = b - xa is orthogonal to a leads to the equation x = a-transpose times b divided by a-transpose times a, which is the scalar formula for the projection coefficient in one dimension.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame captions show two arrows labeled a and b on blackboard with projection diagram
Derivation of x = a^T b / a^T a from the perpendicularity condition

question: When the blackboard shows the equation p = a^T b / a^T a, what happens to the projection p if vector b is doubled, and what happens if vector a is doubled instead?
answer: According to the lecture, if vector b is doubled the projection p also doubles because b appears linearly in the numerator a^T b. However, if vector a is doubled the projection p stays exactly the same because an extra factor of two appears both in the numerator (a^T b gets factor 2 from a) and in the denominator (a^T a gets factor 4), and these cancel out, leaving the projection unchanged.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows equation p = a^T b / a^T a
Discussion of doubling b doubles projection but doubling a leaves projection unchanged

question: The blackboard caption shows properties including C(P) = line through a and rank(P) = 1 for the 1D projection matrix. How does the lecture explain that squaring this matrix P gives P back?
answer: The lecture explains geometrically that applying the projection matrix once moves any vector B onto the line, and if you apply the projection a second time to a point already on the line, that point stays exactly where it is because the projection of a point already on the line is the point itself. Therefore P squared equals P. This is verified algebraically by noting that in the formula P = a a^T / (a^T a), squaring it causes the a^T a in the middle to cancel with the denominator, yielding P again.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame captions show C(P) = line through a, rank(P) = 1 on blackboard
Geometric and algebraic explanation of why P^2 = P

question: The blackboard shows the text 'Why project? Because Ax=b may have no solution.' What does the lecture say is the alternative equation to solve, and why is it guaranteed to be solvable?
answer: The lecture says the alternative is to solve Ax = p instead, where p is the projection of b onto the column space of A. This is guaranteed solvable because Ax always lies in the column space of A by definition, so if we replace b with its projection p onto the column space, then p is already in the column space and the equation Ax = p has a solution. The best solution x-hat is found by the normal equations A-transpose A x = A-transpose b.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows 'Why project? Because Ax=b may have no solution'
Explanation of solving Ax=p instead and why it is solvable

question: The blackboard diagram shows vectors a1, a2, and b with a plane, and later the board shows the normal equations A^T A x = A^T b. How does the lecture connect the geometric perpendicularity of the error e to the plane with these normal equations?
answer: The lecture explains that because the error vector e = b - A x-hat must be perpendicular to the entire plane spanned by a1 and a2, it must be perpendicular to each basis vector individually. This gives two equations: a1^T (b - Ax) = 0 and a2^T (b - Ax) = 0. Stacking these using the matrix A whose columns are a1 and a2, both conditions combine into the single matrix equation A^T (b - Ax) = 0, which rearranges to A^T A x = A^T b, the normal equations.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard diagram shows vectors a1, a2, b with plane and error e perpendicular to plane
Derivation of A^T A x = A^T b from perpendicularity conditions

question: The blackboard shows equations x-hat = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b and the projection matrix P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T. What mistake does the lecturer warn against when simplifying P, and why is that simplification invalid?
answer: The lecturer warns against splitting (A^T A)^{-1} into A^{-1} times (A^T)^{-1}, which would make P simplify to the identity matrix. This is invalid because A is not a square matrix in the main application — it has many more rows than columns, representing an overdetermined system. A non-square matrix does not have a regular inverse, so the formula (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1} cannot be applied. The expression (A^T A)^{-1} must be kept as a single entity.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T and lecturer discusses invalid simplification
Explanation that A is not square and therefore has no inverse

question: The frame caption shows the board displaying 'e in N(A^T), e ⊥ C(A), YES!' What earlier visual in the lecture established that the error vector must be perpendicular to the plane, and how do these two observations together confirm the geometric picture?
answer: Earlier in the lecture, the blackboard showed a diagram with vectors a1, a2, and b where the error vector e = b - p was drawn as perpendicular to the plane of a1 and a2. The lecture established geometrically that this perpendicularity is the crucial fact that determines the projection. Later, the board confirmation 'e in N(A^T), e ⊥ C(A), YES!' shows algebraically that e satisfying A^T e = 0 (being in the null space of A-transpose) is identical to e being perpendicular to the column space of A. These two facts — the geometric diagram and the four-subspace relationship — confirm each other perfectly.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard diagram showing e perpendicular to plane of a1 and a2
Blackboard shows e in N(A^T) and e perpendicular to C(A) with YES confirmation

question: According to the frame caption for the opening of the lecture, what four topics are written on the blackboard as the agenda for this lecture?
answer: According to the frame caption, the blackboard at the opening of the lecture shows four items written in white chalk: 'Linear Algebra Lecture 15' as the heading, followed by 'Projections!' in a larger font with an exclamation mark, then 'Least Squares', and finally 'PROJECTION MATRIX', indicating these are the main topics to be covered in Lecture 15.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard with Linear Algebra Lecture 15, Projections!, Least Squares, PROJECTION MATRIX

question: What does the frame caption describe as being written on the blackboard regarding the reason for projection and the alternative approach when Ax = b has no solution?
answer: According to the frame caption, the blackboard shows the text 'Why project? Because Ax=b may have no solution' followed by 'Solve Ax=p instead' and 'proj of b onto col space', indicating that when the original system Ax = b cannot be solved exactly, the recommended alternative is to project b onto the column space of A and solve the resulting equation instead.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard with Why project, Because Ax=b may have no solution, Solve Ax=p instead, proj of b onto col space

question: Why does the lecture treat the projection matrix P = a a^T / (a^T a) as a rank-one matrix in the 1D case, and how does this connect to the column space of that matrix?
answer: The lecture explains that the projection matrix P = a a^T / (a^T a) is a rank-one matrix because it is in the form of a column times a row — specifically, the column vector a multiplied by the row vector a^T, divided by the scalar a^T a. A column times a row always produces a rank-one matrix. The column space of this rank-one matrix is spanned by just that single column vector a, which is exactly the line onto which we are projecting. So the column space of P is the line through a, which confirms the geometric meaning: multiplying any vector B by P projects it onto that line.

ground_truth_spans.description: Derivation showing projection matrix P = a a^T / a^T a
Explanation that P is rank-one with column space equal to the line through a

question: How does the lecture explain that the error vector e in the multi-dimensional projection problem belongs to the null space of A-transpose, and why does this confirm the geometric picture from previous lectures?
answer: The lecture derives the normal equations A^T (b - A x-hat) = 0, which means A^T e = 0, placing e in the null space of A-transpose. The lecture then notes that from the four fundamental subspaces studied in the previous lecture, the null space of A-transpose is orthogonal to the column space of A. Since e being in the null space of A-transpose means e is perpendicular to the column space of A, this is precisely the geometric condition that the error vector must be perpendicular to the plane (column space) onto which we projected. The algebraic and geometric perspectives thus confirm each other perfectly.

ground_truth_spans.description: Derivation showing A^T e = 0 so e is in null space of A^T
Connection to four fundamental subspaces and orthogonality of null space of A^T with column space of A

question: In the least squares line-fitting example, what are the three data points given, what system of equations Ax = b results from trying to fit a line b = C + Dt through them, and why does that system have no solution?
answer: The three data points given are (t=1, b=1), (t=2, b=2), and (t=3, b=2). Fitting a line b = C + Dt through these three points produces the system: C + D = 1, C + 2D = 2, and C + 3D = 2. This system has no solution because there is no single line that passes exactly through all three points — the right-hand side values 1, 2, 2 are inconsistent with lying perfectly on any straight line. The matrix A has the form with ones in the first column and t-values 1, 2, 3 in the second column, making it a 3-by-2 overdetermined system.

ground_truth_spans.description: Data points at t=1,2,3 with b=1,2,2 given for line fitting problem
Three equations C+D=1, C+2D=2, C+3D=2 stated with conclusion of no solution

question: What specific algebraic reason does the lecturer give for why the formula x = a^T b / a^T a in one dimension changes to x-hat = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b in higher dimensions, rather than simply dividing by a scalar?
answer: The lecturer explains that in one dimension, a^T a is just a number — it is the scalar dot product of the column vector a with itself, equal to the squared length of a. So dividing by it is straightforward. However, in higher dimensions, A^T A becomes an n-by-n matrix rather than a scalar, and you cannot divide by a matrix; instead you must multiply by the inverse of that matrix. This is why the formula requires (A^T A)^{-1} rather than simple division.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that a^T a is a scalar in 1D but A^T A is an n-by-n matrix in higher dimensions requiring inverse

question: What does the lecturer say happens to the projection matrix formula P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T in the special case where A is a square invertible matrix, and what does this imply about the projection?
answer: The lecturer explains that if A is a square invertible n-by-n matrix, its column space is all of R^n. In this case the projection matrix P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T simplifies to the identity matrix I. This is because when A is square and invertible, you can split (A^T A)^{-1} into A^{-1} (A^T)^{-1}, causing everything to cancel to give A times A^{-1} times (A^T)^{-1} times A^T which equals the identity. The implication is that projecting onto the entire space leaves every vector B unchanged, so Pb = b.

ground_truth_spans.description: Discussion of projection matrix when A is square invertible giving identity matrix and projection onto all of R^n
